serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Buttered Sotong is approximately 200 g (one plate), providing around 464 kcal, 28 g protein, 32 g fat, and 12 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Squid, mixed species, raw (FDC ID 175168) and Butter, salted (FDC ID 173410); adapted composite estimate for deep-fried buttered squid preparation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Buttered Sotong exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA FDC squid (raw, FDC ID 175168) adjusted for deep-frying fat absorption (~8–10% fat uptake) and butter sauce addition, cross-referenced against the input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Input per_100g values are within plausible range for this preparation and have been retained with minor validation. Calcium, iron, cholesterol, trans_fat, and vitamin_c values are sourced from USDA FDC squid and butter entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on battered fried seafood
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Buttered Sotong. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous battered deep-fried seafood entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. The wheat flour batter is the primary glycaemic driver; squid itself contributes negligible carbohydrate. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 12) / 100 = 6.6 for a 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 380 mg/100 g and saturated fat 8 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g per occasion, request reduced butter and salt during preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes to keep total meal sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~55) from wheat flour batter; low carbohydrate load at 6 g/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Buttered Sotong has a low carbohydrate content of 6 g/100 g and a low estimated GI of 55, yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 6.6 per 200 g serving; individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid pairing with white rice or other refined carbohydrates to prevent cumulative glycaemic load elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 8 g/100 g, trans fat 0.2 g/100 g, and cholesterol 180 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high saturated fat (8 g/100 g), trans fat (0.2 g/100 g), and cholesterol (180 mg/100 g) from butter and deep-frying poses significant cardiovascular risk; individuals with CVD should limit consumption to small occasional portions and request preparation with minimal butter and a non-hydrogenated frying oil.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 8 g/100 g exceeds 5 g/100 g RA threshold; deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Buttered Sotong due to high saturated fat content and AGE formation from deep-frying, both of which promote joint inflammation; substituting butter with olive oil and requesting pan-frying rather than deep-frying may reduce but not eliminate the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Buttered Sotong is a popular Singaporean deep-fried squid dish coated in a crispy wheat flour batter and finished with a rich, aromatic butter sauce typically made with butter, evaporated milk or coconut milk, curry leaves, chilli padi, and egg yolk. The squid (sotong in Malay) is first deep-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in the butter sauce, resulting in a dish that is simultaneously crunchy, creamy, savoury, and mildly spicy. It is commonly served as a sharing appetiser or side dish in Singaporean zi char (煮炒) restaurants and hawker-style eateries. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 232 kcal, 14 g protein, 16 g fat (of which 8 g is saturated), 6 g carbohydrate, 2 g sugar, 0.4 g dietary fibre, and 380 mg sodium. The high saturated fat and sodium content make it a dish of concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The low carbohydrate content (6 g/100 g) and low estimated glycaemic index (~55) mean the glycaemic load per standard 200 g serving is approximately 6.6, which is manageable for most individuals with type 2 diabetes when not paired with other high-carbohydrate foods. The dish is generally considered halal. Cholesterol content is notable at 180 mg/100 g due to squid's naturally high cholesterol profile.
common_user_queries: How many calories in buttered sotong?
Is buttered sotong high in sodium?
Is buttered sotong suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Buttered Sotong exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FDC squid (raw) and butter entries, adjusted for deep-frying and butter sauce preparation. Input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are within the plausible range for this preparation and have been retained after cross-validation. GI is an analogous estimate from battered fried seafood in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient profile, and moisture data for this specific Singaporean buttered deep-fried squid preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. Cultural metadata in the input record contained errors (primary_cuisine listed as 'Filipino'; ethnic_tags listed 'Noodle', 'Coffee', 'Cake' — all inconsistent with this dish); these have been corrected to Singaporean cuisine. Duplicate portion entries in the input (three entries all labelled '1 plate' with different weights) have been resolved by selecting 200 g as the canonical default serving size, consistent with a typical zi char restaurant portion. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) did not directly support the nutrient values claimed; USDA FoodData Central has been used as the approved adapted source. Trans fat is reported at 0.2 g/100 g from the input record; this is plausible given butter content and has been retained.
